title: SEGMENT 14 — Bispecific ADCs and multi-payload designs

lines.text: SEGMENT 14 — Bispecific ADCs and multi-payload designs
Let's spend a segment on the design frontier — the next generation of ADCs that go beyond the simple antibody-linker-payload architecture.
Two ideas dominate this frontier: bispecific antibodies as the targeting component, and multi-payload designs that carry more than one type of warhead.
Bispecific ADCs use an antibody that recognizes two different targets simultaneously.
The conventional ADC antibody binds one antigen — HER2, or CD30, or TROP2.
A bispecific antibody is engineered to have one arm that binds one target and another arm that binds a different target.
As an ADC, this can do several things that monospecific ADCs cannot.
One application is to require dual antigen expression for ADC uptake.

If your antibody only binds cells that express both target A and target B, you can be much more selective for tumor cells, because the combination of two markers may be uniquely cancer-associated even when each marker individually is also found on normal tissues.
The two arms have to grab simultaneously to internalize the drug.
Healthy cells with only one of the two markers don't pull in enough ADC to be killed.
Another application is to use one arm for tumor targeting and the other arm to drive internalization.
Some good cancer targets — proteins that are overexpressed on tumors — don't internalize efficiently when an antibody binds them.
They just sit on the cell surface.
By pairing such a target with a second, more endocytically active receptor on the same cell, you can get the tumor selectivity of the first target plus the internalization kinetics of the second.
A third application is to deliver payload to one cell while simultaneously engaging an immune cell.

We're going to come back to immunostimulatory payloads in Segment 18, but you can imagine an ADC where one arm targets a tumor antigen, the other arm engages a T cell receptor, and the payload is a checkpoint inhibitor or a cytokine.
These designs blur the line between ADC and bispecific T-cell engager.
Multi-payload ADCs carry more than one type of warhead.
The simplest version is an ADC that carries two different payloads — say, a tubulin inhibitor and a topoisomerase inhibitor — so that the killing mechanism is combinatorial.
A tumor cell that has become resistant to one mechanism may still be vulnerable to the other.
This is conceptually analogous to combination chemotherapy, but in a single molecule.
The chemistry of multi-payload conjugation is tricky.

You need to install two different conjugation sites on the antibody, each chemically orthogonal to the other, so that you can attach payload A to site one and payload B to site two without cross-reactions.
Site-specific conjugation chemistry — which we covered in Segment 11 — is essentially required for this.
You can't reliably make multi-payload ADCs with stochastic conjugation; the mixtures get hopelessly complex.
Several multi-payload ADCs are in early clinical development.
The data is still preliminary, but the concept is promising.
If you can get clean combinatorial killing in a single targeted molecule, you may be able to outpace resistance mechanisms that affect any single payload.
Beyond these two specific frontiers, the broader design philosophy is becoming modular.
Think of the antibody, the linker, and the payload as three independent dimensions, each with multiple options.
The art of next-generation ADC design is choosing the right combination for each specific clinical problem.
We'll come back to that toolkit framing in the final segment.
